---
title: "Grocery Pricing Overview"
subtitle: "Coverage statistics and a short walkthrough of the Streamlit app"
author: "Grocery Pricing Project"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    theme: flatly
    df_print: paged
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
root_dir <- getwd()

if (!file.exists(file.path(root_dir, "scripts", "run-r.sh"))) {
  candidate_root <- normalizePath(file.path(root_dir, ".."), winslash = "/", mustWork = FALSE)
  if (file.exists(file.path(candidate_root, "scripts", "run-r.sh"))) {
    root_dir <- candidate_root
  } else {
    stop("Run this document from the repo root or from analysis/.", call. = FALSE)
  }
}

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(htmltools)
library(knitr)
library(readr)
library(scales)
library(tidyr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.width = 10,
  fig.height = 5.5,
  out.width = "100%"
)

theme_set(
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.title.position = "plot",
      plot.caption.position = "plot"
    )
)

data_dir <- file.path(root_dir, "analysis", "grocery_pricing_for_friends_data")
asset_dir <- file.path(root_dir, "analysis", "grocery_pricing_for_friends_assets")

required_files <- c(
  "snapshot_metadata.csv",
  "dataset_overview.csv",
  "rows_by_year.csv",
  "rows_by_location_type.csv",
  "year_location_type.csv",
  "top_chains_by_rows.csv",
  "hannaford_example_row.csv",
  "confirmed_overview.csv",
  "confirmed_coverage_counts.csv",
  "confirmed_by_task_group.csv",
  "largest_confirmed_stores.csv",
  "confirmed_row_distribution.csv",
  "qaqc_overview.csv"
)

missing_required <- required_files[!file.exists(file.path(data_dir, required_files))]
if (length(missing_required) > 0) {
  stop(
    paste(
      "Missing report inputs:",
      paste(missing_required, collapse = ", "),
      "Run python scripts/generate_publication_inputs.py first."
    ),
    call. = FALSE
  )
}

snapshot_metadata <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "snapshot_metadata.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
dataset_overview <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "dataset_overview.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
rows_by_year <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "rows_by_year.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
rows_by_location_type <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "rows_by_location_type.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
year_location_type <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "year_location_type.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
top_chains_by_rows <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "top_chains_by_rows.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
hannaford_example_row <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "hannaford_example_row.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
confirmed_overview <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "confirmed_overview.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
confirmed_coverage_counts <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "confirmed_coverage_counts.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
confirmed_by_task_group <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "confirmed_by_task_group.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
largest_confirmed_stores <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "largest_confirmed_stores.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
confirmed_row_distribution <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "confirmed_row_distribution.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
qaqc_overview <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "qaqc_overview.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

value_for <- function(frame, metric_name) {
  frame %>% filter(metric == metric_name) %>% pull(value) %>% .[[1]]
}

fmt_num <- function(x, digits = 0) {
  format(round(as.numeric(x), digits), big.mark = ",", trim = TRUE, nsmall = digits)
}

fmt_pct <- function(x, digits = 1) {
  percent(as.numeric(x), accuracy = 10^-digits)
}

fmt_snapshot <- function(x) {
  format(as.POSIXct(x, tz = "UTC"), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M UTC")
}

render_table <- function(data, caption = NULL, digits = NULL) {
  args <- list(
    x = data,
    format = "html",
    caption = caption,
    table.attr = 'class="table table-striped table-bordered table-hover table-condensed" style="width:auto; margin-bottom: 22px;"'
  )
  if (!is.null(digits)) {
    args$digits <- digits
  }
  do.call(knitr::kable, args)
}

friendly_task_group <- function(x) {
  out <- sub("^task_group_", "", x)
  out <- gsub("_", " ", out)
  tools::toTitleCase(out)
}

metric_card <- function(label, value, note = NULL) {
  note_html <- if (is.null(note)) {
    ""
  } else {
    paste0("<div class=\"metric-note\">", htmlEscape(note), "</div>")
  }

  paste0(
    "<div class=\"metric-card\">",
    "<div class=\"metric-label\">", htmlEscape(label), "</div>",
    "<div class=\"metric-value\">", htmlEscape(value), "</div>",
    note_html,
    "</div>"
  )
}

location_palette <- c(
  "chain-wide" = "#8b5cf6",
  "store-specific" = "#0f766e",
  "city-level" = "#2563eb",
  "unknown" = "#94a3b8"
)

snapshot_table <- snapshot_metadata %>%
  transmute(
    Source = source_name,
    File = path,
    `Snapshot (UTC)` = vapply(snapshot_utc, fmt_snapshot, character(1)),
    `Rows in report scope` = comma(rows_in_report_scope),
    Notes = notes
  )

all_rows <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "price_rows"))
all_chains <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "unique_chains"))
all_sources <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "unique_source_files"))
non_sale_rows <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "non_sale_rows"))
sale_rows <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "sale_rows"))
all_cited <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "rows_missing_wayback_url")) == 0
full_six_year_sources <- as.numeric(value_for(dataset_overview, "sources_with_all_6_years"))

registry_entries <- as.numeric(value_for(confirmed_overview, "registry_entries"))
full_confirmed <- as.numeric(value_for(confirmed_overview, "full_2020_2025_entries"))
partial_confirmed <- as.numeric(value_for(confirmed_overview, "partial_entries"))
recorded_full_confirmed_rows <- as.numeric(value_for(confirmed_overview, "full_coverage_recorded_total_rows"))
recorded_full_confirmed_non_sale <- as.numeric(value_for(confirmed_overview, "full_coverage_recorded_non_sale_rows"))
blank_locations <- as.numeric(value_for(confirmed_overview, "blank_location_fields"))
qaqc_flagged_total <- as.numeric(value_for(qaqc_overview, "flagged_files_total"))
qaqc_flagged_in_parquet <- as.numeric(value_for(qaqc_overview, "flagged_files_present_in_parquet"))

rows_by_year <- rows_by_year %>% mutate(year = factor(year, levels = year))
confirmed_row_distribution$row_band <- factor(
  confirmed_row_distribution$row_band,
  levels = c("1-99", "100-999", "1,000-9,999", "10,000-49,999", "50,000+")
)

```

```{r css, results='asis'}
cat(
  "<style>
  .metric-grid {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: repeat(3, minmax(0, 1fr));
    gap: 14px;
    margin: 18px 0 26px 0;
  }
  .metric-card {
    border: 1px solid #dbe4ea;
    border-radius: 12px;
    background: #f8fbfd;
    padding: 16px 18px;
  }
  .metric-label {
    color: #48616f;
    font-size: 0.95rem;
    margin-bottom: 8px;
  }
  .metric-value {
    color: #123244;
    font-size: 1.9rem;
    font-weight: 700;
    line-height: 1.1;
  }
  .metric-note {
    color: #5e7684;
    font-size: 0.88rem;
    margin-top: 8px;
  }
  .callout {
    border-left: 5px solid #2c7fb8;
    background: #f4f9fc;
    padding: 14px 16px;
    margin: 18px 0;
  }
  @media (max-width: 960px) {
    .metric-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
  }
  </style>"
)
```

# Overview

This document is a data-first overview of the grocery pricing archive currently powering the Streamlit dashboard. It is intended to answer two different questions:

1. How much pricing data is currently in the app-facing analytic snapshot?
2. How many stores have been strong enough to count as confirmed multi-year coverage cases of the OFRA basket?


## Collection Methodology

The grocery pricing dataset is scraped from Internet Archive records of SNAP-eligible grocery store chains in the United States. 

## What the Raw Data Looks Like

Each row in the dataset is a price at a specific point in time for a specific item at a specific chain. The best way to see that is to line up an archived grocery page with one extracted row from the data.

The screenshot below shows a Hannaford soups page captured by the Wayback Machine on April 11, 2020. One of the visible items is `Maruchan Instant Beef Flavor Noodle Soup Cup`, which also appears as a row app dataset.

```{r hannaford-source-screenshot, fig.cap="", fig.height=8.2}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(asset_dir, "hannaford_wayback_source.png"))
```

```{r hannaford-example-row}
hannaford_example_table <- hannaford_example_row %>%
  mutate(
    price = dollar(price),
    sale = ifelse(sale, "TRUE", "FALSE")
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Timestamp = timestamp,
    Chain = chain,
    Location = location,
    Product = product_name,
    Price = price,
    Unit = unit,
    Sale = sale,
    Category = category,
    Year = year,
    `Location type` = location_type,
    `Wayback URL` = wayback_url
  )

render_table(hannaford_example_table, caption = "Extracted row corresponding to the visible Hannaford product example")
```


```{r metric-cards, results='asis'}
cards <- c(
  metric_card("App-facing price rows (2020-2025)", fmt_num(all_rows), "Inclusive of sale and non-sale data."),
  metric_card("Unique chains", fmt_num(all_chains), "Distinct chain names in the analytic snapshot."),
  metric_card("Unique source files", fmt_num(all_sources), "Distinct contributing CSV sources represented in the parquet.")
)

cat(paste0("<div class=\"metric-grid\">", paste(cards, collapse = ""), "</div>"))
```

```{r snapshot-callout, results='asis'}
cat(
  sprintf(
    "<div class='callout'><strong>Important caveat.</strong> These values are computed as of April 9, 2026. Actual app counts may have changed as additional data is collected</code>.</div>"
  )
)
```

# App-Facing Dataset Coverage

The analytic snapshot currently contains **`r fmt_num(all_rows)` price rows** from **`r fmt_num(all_chains)` chains** and **`r fmt_num(all_sources)` source files** in the `2020-2025` window. Every row in this snapshot has a nonblank Wayback citation URL.

The year-by-year distribution is not flat. Coverage rises sharply in `2024` and `2025`, but the archive still contains meaningful volume in every year back to `2020`.

```{r rows-by-year-plot, fig.height=4.8}
ggplot(rows_by_year, aes(x = year, y = rows)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7fb8", width = 0.72) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(rows)), vjust = -0.3, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))) +
  labs(
    title = "Price rows by year in the app-facing snapshot",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Rows"
  )
```

```{r rows-by-year-table}
rows_by_year_table <- rows_by_year %>%
  mutate(
    rows = comma(rows),
    unique_chains = comma(unique_chains),
    unique_sources = comma(unique_sources),
    median_price = dollar(median_price),
    non_sale_rows = comma(non_sale_rows),
    sale_rows = comma(sale_rows)
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Year = year,
    Rows = rows,
    Chains = unique_chains,
    Sources = unique_sources,
    `Median price` = median_price,
    `Non-sale rows` = non_sale_rows,
    `Sale rows` = sale_rows
  )

render_table(rows_by_year_table, caption = "App-facing coverage by year")
```

## Sale status and location specificity

Most app-facing rows are marked non-sale, but the archive still contains a sizable sale layer. The location story is more mixed: a large share of rows are chain-wide, while store-specific and city-level records make up the strongest part of the map-oriented experience.

```{r location-type-plot, fig.height=4.5}
ggplot(rows_by_location_type, aes(x = reorder(location_type, rows), y = rows, fill = location_type)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(rows)), hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = location_palette) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.14))) +
  labs(
    title = "Rows by location type",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Rows"
  )
```

```{r year-location-type-plot, fig.height=5.1}
ggplot(year_location_type, aes(x = factor(year), y = rows, fill = location_type)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = location_palette) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "How location-type coverage changes by year",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Rows",
    fill = "Location type"
  )
```

The heaviest row-producing sources in the app-facing snapshot are not all equally clean or equally mission-qualified. Some are excellent store-specific datasets; others are large but chain-wide or QA-heavy. That is why raw app coverage and confirmed-store counts should not be treated as the same thing.

```{r top-chains-table}
top_chains_table <- top_chains_by_rows %>%
  transmute(
    Chain = chain,
    Rows = comma(rows),
    `Years represented` = unique_years,
    `Location types` = location_types
  )

render_table(top_chains_table, caption = "Largest chains in the app-facing snapshot")
```

# How to Use the App

The Streamlit app is best understood as a guided way to move from broad coverage to specific evidence.

## Step 1: Start on the Pricing Map

Use the left sidebar to narrow the dataset before reading any statistics. The most important controls are:

- product categories or baskets
- sale status
- location precision
- year range

The map and the right-hand directory panel update together.

```{r screenshot-pricing-map, fig.cap="Pricing Map. Start here: set the filters in the left sidebar, then use the map and directory panel together to decide which parts of the archive you want to inspect.", fig.height=7.8}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(asset_dir, "app_pricing_map.png"))
```

## Step 2: Scroll down to the aggregate statistics

Once the filters are set, the Pricing Map tab also acts like a summary dashboard. This is the quickest way to see how many rows, chains, and products survive the current filter choices, along with a year-over-year median price view.

```{r screenshot-aggregate, fig.cap="Pricing Map with the aggregate statistics section visible. This is the fastest way to turn the current filter settings into a quantitative summary.", fig.height=7.8}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(asset_dir, "app_aggregate_statistics.png"))
```

## Step 3: Use Product View to rank what is actually available

The Product View tab shifts attention away from geography and toward item-level breadth. It is useful for asking which product names have the most data points, how many years they cover, and how widely they appear across chains and locations.

```{r screenshot-product-view, fig.cap="Product View. After tightening the sidebar filters, this tab is useful for ranking products by observations, chain count, years covered, and price levels.", fig.height=7.8}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(asset_dir, "app_product_view.png"))
```

## Step 4: Use the Harter House tab as a guided case study

The Harter House case study is the most presentation-ready part of the app. It turns a single chain into a narrative example, showing geography, bundle structure, and time-series evidence in one place.

```{r screenshot-harter, fig.cap="Harter House Case Study. This tab is the clearest single example of how the archive can be turned into an interpretable historical pricing story.", fig.height=7.8}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(asset_dir, "app_harter_house_case_study.png"))
```

# Key Caveats

Several caveats matter for any public reading of the archive:

1. **Data collection is ongoing.** This report reflects current collection status at time of writing. The app dataset may have been added or modified subsequent to report publication.
2. **Geographic precision varies.** The app contains chain-wide, city-level, unknown-location, and partially qualified material alongside the strongest store-specific records.
3. **Quality review is still active.** The current QAQC summary flags **`r fmt_num(qaqc_flagged_total)` files** overall, and **`r fmt_num(qaqc_flagged_in_parquet)` of those flagged basenames** still appear in the current dataset snapshot.
4. **Some very large sources are not the strongest inflation evidence.** Large chain-wide or noisy sources can dominate raw row counts while still being weaker than smaller, cleaner store-specific series.

# Bottom Line

The archive is already large enough to be more than a proof of concept.

- At the app level, it currently exposes **`r fmt_num(all_rows)` price rows** from **`r fmt_num(all_chains)` chains** and **`r fmt_num(all_sources)` source files** in the `2020-2025` window.
- At the stricter registry level, it currently lists **`r fmt_num(full_confirmed)` full-coverage confirmed stores** out of **`r fmt_num(registry_entries)` total entries**.
- The app is already usable as an exploratory tool: start with the Pricing Map, read the aggregate statistics, move to Product View for item-level breadth, and use Harter House as a model case study.

The main caution is not that the archive lacks data. It is that the archive contains layers of data with different strengths. **The best way to use the app is to keep those layers explicit**.
